title: Volume Rendering of Neural Implicit Surfaces

## **Volume Rendering of Neural Implicit Surfaces**

**Lior Yariv**[1] **Jiatao Gu**[2] **Yoni Kasten**[1] **Yaron Lipman**[1] _[,]_[2]

1Weizmann Institute of Science 2Facebook AI Research

## **Abstract**

Neural volume rendering became increasingly popular recently due to its success in synthesizing novel views of a scene from a sparse set of input images. So far, the geometry learned by neural volume rendering techniques was modeled using a generic density function. Furthermore, the geometry itself was extracted using an arbitrary level set of the density function leading to a noisy, often low fidelity reconstruction. The goal of this paper is to improve geometry representation and reconstruction in neural volume rendering. We achieve that by modeling the volume density as a function of the geometry. This is in contrast to previous work modeling the geometry as a function of the volume density. In more detail, we define the volume density function as Laplace’s cumulative distribution function (CDF) applied to a signed distance function (SDF) representation. This simple density representation has three benefits: (i) it provides a useful inductive bias to the geometry learned in the neural volume rendering process; (ii) it facilitates a bound on the opacity approximation error, leading to an accurate sampling of the viewing ray. Accurate sampling is important to provide a precise coupling of geometry and radiance; and (iii) it allows efficient unsupervised disentanglement of shape and appearance in volume rendering. Applying this new density representation to challenging scene multiview datasets produced high quality geometry reconstructions, outperforming relevant baselines. Furthermore, switching shape and appearance between scenes is possible due to the disentanglement of the two.

## **1 Introduction**

Volume rendering [18] is a set of techniques that renders volume _density_ in _radiance fields_ by the so called volume rendering integral. It has recently been shown that representing both the density and radiance fields as neural networks can lead to excellent prediction of novel views by learning only from a sparse set of input images. This neural volume rendering approach, presented in [21] and developed by its follow-ups [34, 2] approximates the integral as alpha-composition in a differentiable way, allowing to learn simultaneously both from input images. Although this coupling indeed leads to good generalization of novel viewing directions, the density part is not as successful in faithfully predicting the scene’s actual geometry, often producing noisy, low fidelity geometry approximation.

We propose VolSDF to devise a different model for the density in neural volume rendering, leading to better approximation of the scene’s geometry while maintaining the quality of view synthesis. The key idea is to represent the density as a function of the signed distance to the scene’s surface, see Figure 1. Such density function enjoys several benefits. First, it guarantees the existence of a well-defined surface that generates the density. This provides a useful inductive bias for disentangling density and radiance fields, which in turn provides a more accurate geometry approximation. Second, we show this density formulation allows bounding the approximation error of the opacity along rays. This bound is used to sample the viewing ray so to provide a faithful coupling of density and radiance field in the volume rendering integral. E.g., without such a bound the computed radiance along a ray (pixel color) can potentially miss or extend surface parts leading to incorrect radiance approximation.

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

Figure 1: VolSDF: given a set of input images (left) we learn a volumetric density (center-left, sliced) defined by a signed distance function (center-right, sliced) to produce a neural rendering (right). This definition of density facilitates high quality geometry reconstruction (gray surfaces, middle).

A closely related line of research, often referred to as neural implicit surfaces [22, 38, 14], have been focusing on representing the scene’s geometry implicitly using a neural network, making the surface rendering process differentiable. The main drawback of these methods is their requirement of masks that separate objects from the background. Also, learning to render surfaces directly tends to grow extraneous parts due to optimization problems, which are avoided by volume rendering. In a sense, our work combines the best of both worlds: _volume rendering with neural implicit surfaces_ .

We demonstrate the efficacy of VolSDF by reconstructing surfaces from the DTU [12] and BlendedMVS [37] datasets. VolSDF produces more accurate surface reconstructions compared to NeRF [21] and NeRF++ [39], and comparable reconstruction compared to IDR [38], while avoiding the use of object masks. Furthermore, we show disentanglement results with our method, i.e., switching the density and radiance fields of different scenes, which is shown to fail in NeRF-based models.

## **2 Related work**

**Neural Scene Representation & Rendering** Implicit functions are traditionally adopted in modeling 3D scenes [24, 11, 4]. Recent studies have been focusing on model implicit functions with multi-layer perceptron (MLP) due to its expressive representation power and low memory foot-print, including scene (geometry & appearance) representation [9, 20, 19, 23, 25, 29, 36, 28, 35] and free-view rendering [33, 16, 30, 26, 17, 21, 15, 39, 34, 2]. In particular, NeRF [21] has opened up a line of research (see [6] for an overview) combining neural implicit functions together with volume rendering to achieve photo-realistic rendering results. However, it is non-trivial to find a proper threshold to extract surfaces from the predicted density, and the recovered geometry is far from satisfactory. Furthermore, sampling of points along a ray for rendering a pixel is done using an opacity function that is approximated from another network without any guarantee for correct approximation.

**Multi-view 3D Reconstruction** Image-based 3D surface reconstruction (multi-view stereo) has been a longstanding problem in the past decades. Classical multi-view stereo approaches are generally either depth-based [1, 31, 8, 7] or voxel-based [5, 3, 32]. For instance, in COLMAP [31] (a typical depth-based method) image features are extracted and matched across different views to estimate depth. Then the predicted depth maps are fused to obtain dense point clouds. To obtain the surface, an additional meshing step e.g. Poisson surface reconstruction [13] is applied. However, these methods with complex pipelines may accumulate errors at each stage and usually result in incomplete 3D models, especially for non-Lambertian surfaces as they can not handle view dependent colors. On the contrary, although it produces complete models by directly modeling objects in a volume, voxel-based approaches are limited to low resolution due to high memory consumption. Recently, neural-based approaches such as DVR [22], IDR [38], NLR [14] have also been proposed to reconstruct scene geometry from multi-view images. However, these methods require accurate object masks and appropriate weight initialization due to the difficulty of propagating gradients.

Independently from and concurrently with our work here, [27] also use implicit surface representation incorporated into volume rendering. In particular, they replace the local transparency function with an occupancy network [19]. This allows adding surface smoothing term to the loss, improving the quality of the resulting surfaces. Differently from their approach, we use signed distance representation, regularized with an Eikonal loss [38, 10] without any explicit smoothing term. Furthermore, we show that the choice of using signed distance allows bounding the opacity approximation error, facilitating the approximation of the volume rendering integral for the suggested family of densities.

## **3 Method**

In this section we introduce a novel parameterization for volume density, defined as transformed signed distance function. Then we show how this definition facilitates the volume rendering process. In particular, we derive a bound of the error in the opacity approximation and consequently devise a sampling procedure for approximating the volume rendering integral.

## **3.1 Density as transformed SDF**

Let the set Ω _⊂_ R[3] represent the space occupied by some object in R[3] , and _M_ = _∂_ Ω its boundary surface. We denote by **1** Ω the Ω indicator function, and by _d_ Ω the Signed Distance Function (SDF) to its boundary _M_ ,

where _∥·∥_ is the standard Euclidean 2-norm. In neural volume rendering the volume _density σ_ : R[3] _→_ R+ is a scalar volumetric function, where _σ_ ( _**x**_ ) is the rate that light is occluded at point _**x**_ ; _σ_ is called density since it is proportional to the particle count per unit volume at _**x**_ [18]. In previous neural volumetric rendering approaches [21, 15, 39], the density function, _σ_ , was modeled with a general-purpose Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP). In this work we suggest to model the density using a certain transformation of a learnable Signed Distance Function (SDF) _d_ Ω, namely

_σ_ ( _**x**_ ) = _α_ Ψ _β_ ( _−d_ Ω( _**x**_ )) _,_ (2)

where _α, β >_ 0 are learnable parameters, and Ψ _β_ is the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of the Laplace distribution with zero mean and _β_ scale (i.e., mean absolute deviation, which is intuitively the _L_ 1 version of the standard deviation),

Figure 1 (center left and right) depicts an example of such a density and SDF. As can be readily checked from this definition, as _β_ approach zero, the density _σ_ converges to a scaled indicator function of Ω, that is _σ → α_ **1** Ω for all points _**x** ∈_ Ω _\ M_ .

Intuitively, the density _σ_ models a homogeneous object with a constant density _α_ that smoothly decreases near the object’s boundary, where the smoothing amount is controlled by _β_ . The benefit in defining the density as in equation 2 is two-fold: First, it provides a useful inductive bias for the surface geometry _M_ , and provides a principled way to reconstruct the surface, i.e., as the zero level-set of _d_ Ω. This is in contrast to previous work where the reconstruction was chosen as an arbitrary level set of the learned density. Second, the particular form of the density as defined in equation 2 facilitates a bound on the error of the _opacity_ (or, equivalently the _transparency_ ) of the rendered volume, a crucial component in the volumetric rendering pipeline. In contrast, such a bound will be hard to devise for a generic MLP densities.

## **3.2 Volume rendering of** _σ_

In this section we review the volume rendering integral and the numerical integration commonly used to approximate it, requiring a set _S_ of sample points per ray. In the following section (Section 3.3), we explore the properties of the density _σ_ and derive a bound on the opacity approximation error along viewing rays. Finally, in Section 3.4 we derive an algorithm for producing a sample _S_ to be used in the volume rendering numerical integration.

In volume rendering we consider a ray _**x**_ emanating from a camera position _**c** ∈_ R[3] in direction _**v** ∈_ R[3] , _∥_ _**v** ∥_ = 1, defined by _**x**_ ( _t_ ) = _**c**_ + _t_ _**v**_ , _t ≥_ 0. In essence, volume rendering is all about approximating the integrated (i.e., summed) light radiance along this ray reaching the camera. There are two important quantities that participate in this computation: the volume’s _opacity O_ , or equivalently, its _transperancy T_ , and the _radiance field L_ .

The _transparency_ function of the volume along a ray _**x**_ , denoted _T_ , indicates, for each _t ≥_ 0, the probability a light particle succeeds traversing the segment [ _**c** ,_ _**x**_ ( _t_ )] without bouncing off,

**Sampling.** Since the PDF _τ_ is typically extremely concentrated near the object’s boundary (see e.g., Figure 3, right) the choice of the sample points _S_ has a crucial effect on the approximation quality of equation 8. One solution is to use an adaptive sample, e.g., _S_ computed with the inverse CDF, i.e., _O[−]_[1] . However, _O_ depends on the density model _σ_ and is not given explicitly. In [21] a second, coarse network was trained specifically for the approximation of the opacity _O_ , and was used for inverse sampling. However, the second network’s density does not necessarily faithfully represents the first network’s density, for which we wish to compute the volume integral. Furthermore, as we show later, one level of sampling could be insufficient to produce an accurate sample _S_ . Using a naive or crude approximation of _O_ would lead to a sub-optimal sample set _S_ that misses, or over extends non-negligible _τ_ values. Consequently, incorrect radiance approximations can occur (i.e., pixel color), potentially harming the learned density-radiance field decomposition. Our solution works with a single density _σ_ , and the sampling _S_ is computed by a sampling algorithm based on an error bound for the opacity approximation. ## **3.4 Sampling algorithm**

In this section we develop an algorithm for computing the sampling _S_ to be used in equation 8. This is done by first utilizing the bound in equation 15 to find samples _T_ so that _O_[�] (via equation 10) provides an _ϵ_ approximation to the true opacity _O_ , where _ϵ_ is a hyper-parameter, that is _BT ,β < ϵ_ . Second, we perform inverse CDF sampling with _O_[ˆ] , as described in Section 3.2.

Note that from Lemma 1 it follows that we can simply choose large enough _n_ to ensure _BT ,β < ϵ_ . However, this would lead to prohibitively large number of samples. Instead, we suggest a simple algorithm to reduce the number of required samples in practice and allows working with a limited budget of sample points. In a nutshell, we start with a uniform sampling _T_ = _T_ 0, and use Lemma 2 to initially set a _β_ + _> β_ that satisfies _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ . Then, we repeatedly upsample _T_ to reduce _β_ + while maintaining _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ . Even though this simple strategy is not guaranteed to converge, we find that _β_ + usually converges to _β_ (typically 85%, see also Figure 3), and even in cases it does not, the algorithm provides _β_ + for which the opacity approximation still maintains an _ϵ_ error. The algorithm is presented below (Algorithm 1).

We initialize _T_ (Line 1 in Algorithm 1) with uniform sampling _T_ 0 = _{ti}[n] i_ =1[, where] _[ t][k]_[= (] _[k][−]_[1)] _n[M] −_ 1[,] _k ∈_ [ _n_ ] (we use _n_ = 128 in our implementation). Given this sampling we next pick _β_ + _> β_ according to Lemma 2 so that the error bound satisfies the required _ϵ_ bound (Line 2 in Algorithm 1).

In order to reduce _β_ + while keep _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ , _n_ samples are added to _T_ (Line 4 in Algorithm 1), where the number of points sampled from each interval is proportional to its current error bound, equation 14. Assuming _T_ was sufficiently upsampled and satisfy _BT ,β_ + _< ϵ_ , we decrease _β_ + towards _β_ . Since the algorithm did not stop we have that _BT ,β > ϵ_ . Therefore the Mean Value Theorem implies the existence of _β⋆ ∈_ ( _β, β_ +) such that _BT ,β⋆_ = _ϵ_ . We use the bisection method (with maximum of 10 iterations) to efficiently search for _β⋆_ and update _β_ + accordingly (Lines 6 and 7 in Algorithm 1). The algorithm runs iteratively until _BT ,β ≤ ϵ_ or a maximal number of 5 iterations is reached. Either way, we use the final _T_ and _β_ + (guaranteed to provide _BT ,β_ + _≤ ϵ_ ) to estimate the current opacity _O_[�] , Line 10 in Algorithm 1). Finally we return a fresh set of _m_ = 64 samples _O_[ˆ] using inverse transform sampling (Line 11 in Algorithm 1). ## **3.5 Training**

Our system consists of two Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLP): (i) _**f** ϕ_ approximating the SDF of the learned geometry, as well as global geometry feature _**z**_ of dimension 256, i.e., _**f** ϕ_ ( _**x**_ ) = ( _d_ ( _**x**_ ) _,_ _**z**_ ( _**x**_ )) _∈_ R[1+256] , where _ϕ_ denotes its learnable parameters; (ii) _Lψ_ ( _**x** ,_ _**n** ,_ _**v** ,_ _**z**_ ) _∈_ R[3] representing the scene’s radiance field with learnable parameters _ψ_ . In addition we have two scalar learnable parameters _α, β ∈_ R. In fact, in our implementation we make the choice _α_ = _β[−]_[1] . We denote by _θ ∈_ R _[p]_ the collection of all learnable parameters of the model, _θ_ = ( _ϕ, ψ, β_ ). To facilitate the learning of high frequency details of the geometry and radiance field, we exploit positional encoding [21] for the position _**x**_ and view direction _**v**_ in the geometry and radiance field. The influence of different positional encoding choices are presented in the supplementary.

Our data consists of a collection of images with camera parameters. From this data we extract pixel level data: for each pixel _p_ we have a triplet ( _Ip,_ _**c** p,_ _**v** p_ ), where _Ip ∈_ R[3] is its intensity (RGB color), _**c** p ∈_ R[3] is its camera location, and _**v** p ∈_ R[3] is the viewing direction (camera to pixel). We train with batches of size 1024 pixels _p_ . _λ_ is a hyper-parameter set to 0 _._ 1 throughout the the experiments. Further implementation details are provided in the supplementary.

## **4 Experiments**

We evaluate our method on the challenging task of multiview 3D surface reconstruction. We use two datasets: DTU [12] and BlendedMVS [37], both containing real objects with different materials that are captured from multiple views. In Section 4.1 we show qualitative and quantitative 3D surface reconstruction results of VolSDF, comparing favorably to relevant baselines. In Section 4.2 we demonstrate that, in contrast to NeRF [21], our model is able to successfully disentangle the geometry and appearance of the captured objects.

## **4.1 Multi-view 3D reconstruction**

**DTU** The DTU [12] dataset contains multi-view image (49 or 64) of different objects with fixed camera and lighting parameters. We evaluate our method on the 15 scans that were selected by [38]. We compare our surface accuracy using the Chamfer _l_ 1 loss (measured in mm) to COLMAP0 (which is watertight reconstruction; COLMAP7 is not watertight and provided only for reference) [31], NeRF [21] and IDR [38], where for fair comparison with IDR we only evaluate the reconstruction inside the visual hull of the objects (defined by the segmentation masks of [38]). We further evaluate the PSNR of our rendering compared to [21]. Quantitative results are presented in Table 1. It can be observed that our method is on par with IDR (that uses object masks for all images) and outperforms NeRF and COLMAP in terms of reconstruction accuracy. Our rendering quality is comparable to NeRF’s.

**BlendedMVS** The BlendedMVS dataset [37] contains a large collection of 113 scenes captured from multiple views. It supplies high quality ground truth 3D models for evaluation, various camera configurations, and a variety of indoor/outdoor real environments. We selected 9 different scenes and used our method to reconstruct the surface of each object. In contrast to the DTU dataset, BlendedMVS scenes have complex backgrounds. Therefore we use NeRF++ [39] as a baseline for this dataset. In Table 2 we present our results compared to NeRF++. Qualitative comparisons are presented in Fig. 5; since the units are unknown in this case we present relative improvement of Chamfer distance (in %) compared to NeRF. Also in this case, we improve NeRF reconstructions considerably, while being on-par in terms of the rendering quality (PSNR).

**Comparison to [38]** IDR [38] is the state of the art 3D surface reconstruction method using implicit representation. However, it suffers from two drawbacks: first, it requires object masks for training, which is a strong supervision signal. Second, since it sets the pixel color based only on the single point of intersection of the corresponding viewing ray, it is more pruned to local minima that Figure 6: IDR extraneous parts. sometimes appear in the form of extraneous surface parts. ## **4.2 Disentanglement of geometry and appearance**

We have tested the disentanglement of scenes to geometry (density) and appearance (radiance field) by switching the radiance fields of two trained scenes. For VolSDF we switched _Lψ_ . For NeRF [21] we note that the radiance field is computed as _Lψ_ ( _**z** ,_ _**v**_ ), where _Lψ_ is a fully connected network with one hidden layer (of width 128 and ReLU activation) and _**z**_ is a feature vector. We tested two versions of NeRF disentanglement: First, by switching the original radiance fields _Lψ_ of trained NeRF networks. Second, by switching the radiance fields of trained NeRF models with an identical radiance field model to ours, namely _Lψ_ ( _**x** ,_ _**n** ,_ _**v** ,_ _**z**_ ). As shown in Figure 7 both versions of NeRF fail to produce a correct disentanglement in these scenes, while VolSDF successfully switches the materials of the two objects. We attribute this to the specific inductive bias injected with the use of the density in equation 2.

## **5 Conclusions**

We introduce VolSDF, a volume rendering framework for implicit neural surfaces. We represent the volume density as a transformed version of the signed distance function to the learned surface geometry. This seemingly simple definition provides a useful inductive bias, allowing disentanglement of geometry (i.e., density) and radiance field, and improves the geometry approximation over previous neural volume rendering techniques. Furthermore, it allows to bound the opacity approximation error leading to high fidelity sampling of the volume rendering integral.

Some limitations of our method present interesting future research opportunities. First, although working well in practice, we do not have a proof of correctness for the sampling algorithm. We believe providing such a proof, or finding a version of this algorithm that has a proof would be a useful contribution. In general, we believe working with bounds in volume rendering could improve learning and disentanglement and push the field forward. Second, representing non-watertight manifolds and/or manifolds with boundaries, such as zero thickness surfaces, is not possible with an SDF. Generalizations such as multiple implicits and unsigned fields could be proven valuable. Third, our current formulation assumes homogeneous density; extending it to more general density models would allow representing a broader class of geometries. Fourth, now that high quality geometries can be learned in an unsupervised manner it will be interesting to learn dynamic geometries and shape spaces directly from collections of images. Lastly, although we don’t see immediate negative societal impact of our work, we do note that accurate geometry reconstruction from images can be used for malice purposes.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a compelling and influential approach to neural volume rendering by grounding the density function in a signed distance function (SDF), which provides strong geometric inductive bias. This leads to improved geometry reconstruction and better disentanglement of shape and appearance, demonstrated on standard multiview datasets. The method is well-motivated, and the core idea—using the Laplace CDF of an SDF to define density—is elegant and impactful in practice.

Overview issues:

- Opacity error bound does not guarantee sampling quality near surfaces: The paper derives a theoretical bound on the opacity approximation error (Theorem 2) and uses it to guide sampling via Algorithm 1. However, the bound in equation (15) depends on both the accumulated density \(\widehat{R}(t_k)\) and the error estimate \(\widehat{E}(t_{k+1})\), which decay exponentially in regions far from the surface. As a result, the bound can be small even if the sampling is coarse near the surface, as long as the transparency is already low. This undermines the claim that the algorithm ensures high-fidelity coupling of geometry and radiance: the sampling may miss fine surface details while still satisfying the \(\epsilon\)-bound. The visualization in Figure 3 shows convergence in the bound, but does not demonstrate that surface-level accuracy is maintained. A tighter, geometry-aware error criterion—such as bounding gradient errors in \(O(t)\) near zero crossings of \(d_\Omega\)—would better justify the sampling strategy.

- Unverified assumption that \(\alpha = \beta^{-1}\) improves training stability: In Section 3.5, the authors state they set \(\alpha = \beta^{-1}\) in implementation but do not justify this choice beyond implying it helps training. This coupling ties the magnitude of the density to the sharpness of the surface transition, which could either help or hurt optimization depending on scene scale and network initialization. Without ablation studies or theoretical motivation, this appears to be a fragile heuristic. For instance, if \(\beta\) decreases during training (sharpening the surface), \(\alpha\) increases unboundedly, potentially causing numerical instability in \(\sigma(x)\). The paper should either provide evidence that this choice improves convergence or treat \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) as independent learnable parameters with proper initialization and regularization.

- Sampling algorithm lacks convergence guarantees and may undersample high-curvature regions: Algorithm 1 adaptively refines sampling based on the opacity error bound, but the refinement criterion (Line 4) distributes new samples according to interval-wise error estimates that depend on \(\exp(-d_i^\star / \beta)\). This term decays rapidly as \(|d_i^\star|\) increases, meaning that regions moderately far from the surface—even if they contain high-curvature or topologically complex geometry—are downweighted. Consequently, the algorithm may undersample areas where the SDF changes rapidly but \(d_i^\star\) is not near zero, such as thin structures or creases. The method also relies on bisection to adjust \(\beta_+\), but there is no proof that \(B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_\star} = \epsilon\) has a solution or that the final \(\mathcal{T}\) yields accurate radiance integration. A comparison with curvature-adaptive sampling or Hessian-based refinement would strengthen the claims.

- Disentanglement claim is not rigorously evaluated: The paper claims that VolSDF enables disentanglement of geometry and appearance, demonstrated via material transfer in Figure 7. However, this is a qualitative experiment with no controls: the NeRF baseline uses a different radiance architecture, and the modified NeRF with normals is not trained from scratch for disentanglement. Moreover, the success of transfer could depend on feature alignment in \(z(x)\), which is not analyzed. A more convincing evaluation would include quantitative metrics such as feature similarity (e.g., LPIPS) between transferred renderings and ground truth materials, or user studies. At minimum, the authors should show that the global feature \(z(x)\) is invariant to appearance changes and sensitive to geometry, which would support the disentanglement claim.

- SDF regularity assumptions conflict with learned MLP outputs: The theoretical results (e.g., Theorem 1) rely on properties of true SDFs, such as \(\|\nabla d_\Omega\| = 1\) almost everywhere. However, the Eikonal regularization in \(\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SDF}}\) only encourages this condition in expectation over samples and cannot guarantee it holds along rays. As a result, the derivative bound in equation (11) may not hold during training or inference, invalidating the opacity error bound. The inset in Section 3.3 shows a geometric construction involving balls \(B_i \cup B_{i+1}\) that assumes the SDF is exact, but no analysis is provided on how network approximation error affects \(d_i^\star\). The method would be more robust if the sampling algorithm used empirical gradient norms from the network rather than theoretical bounds dependent on ideal SDF properties.

- No ablation on opacity error bound tightness: The paper claims the opacity error bound (Theorem 2) ensures faithful coupling of geometry and radiance by guiding sampling, but it does not demonstrate whether the bound is tight in practice. Readers will wonder whether the bound is overly conservative or fails to capture actual integration errors in high-gradient regions. To validate its utility, the authors should compute both the theoretical bound and the empirical opacity error—via fine-grained numerical integration—on a representative ray intersecting a surface, and compare the two across training iterations. Without this, the central mechanism for adaptive sampling lacks empirical grounding.

- Missing comparison to NeRF's hierarchical sampling: The method proposes Algorithm 1 as a replacement for NeRF’s hierarchical volume sampling, yet it never compares the two strategies under the same model capacity or training conditions. A natural question is whether the improvement in geometry comes from the SDF-based density or just better sampling. The authors should implement VolSDF with NeRF’s two-level coarse+fine sampling and report reconstruction accuracy, isolating the contribution of the density formulation from the sampling scheme. This is standard practice in the field and necessary to support the claim of outperforming NeRF.

- No evaluation of disentanglement beyond visual transfer: While Figure 7 shows compelling material swaps, the paper does not establish that the disentanglement is meaningful or consistent across scenes. The global feature $ z(x) $ could encode appearance or geometry in an entangled way despite the SDF prior. To substantiate the claim, the authors should perform a probe experiment: train a classifier to predict object identity or material from $ z(x) $ on held-out scenes and report accuracy. If shape and appearance are truly disentangled, identity should be predictable from $ z $ while material should not, and vice versa when conditioning on $ L_\psi $. This type of analysis is expected for disentanglement claims in modern representation learning.

- Underspecified training dynamics of $\beta$: The sampling algorithm depends on $\beta$ to control the density falloff, and the paper sets $\alpha = \beta^{-1}$ without analyzing how $\beta$ evolves during training. Since $\beta$ affects both the SDF-to-density transformation and the error bound, its value directly impacts sampling quality. The authors should plot $\beta$ over training iterations across multiple seeds and scenes, and correlate it with reconstruction accuracy and PSNR. This is especially important given Lemma 2’s dependence on $\beta$: if $\beta$ drifts too high, sampling may become inefficient. Without this, the practical stability of the method remains unclear.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Revise the opacity error bound to account for surface proximity, ensuring that sampling remains fine near zero-level sets of the SDF, not just where the bound is large.

- Provide ablation or theoretical justification for the choice \(\alpha = \beta^{-1}\), or revert to independent parameters with proper initialization.

- Add analysis showing that the Eikonal-regularized SDF satisfies the assumptions used in Theorem 1, or modify the sampling algorithm to use empirical gradient bounds.

- Strengthen the disentanglement evaluation with controlled experiments, quantitative metrics, or feature space analysis.

- Include a discussion or experiment showing how the algorithm behaves on high-curvature or thin structures, where current sampling may undersample.

comments.title: Opacity error bound does not ensure surface-level sampling fidelity
comments.quote: it facilitates a bound on the opacity approximation error, leading to an accurate sampling of the viewing ray. Accurate sampling is important to provide a precise coupling of geometry and radiance
comments.explanation: The opacity error bound in Theorem 2 depends on accumulated transmittance, which decays exponentially away from the surface. As a result, the bound can be small even if sampling is coarse near the surface—precisely where geometric fidelity matters most. This means fine surface details may be missed while still satisfying the error tolerance. The bound controls global opacity error but does not specifically regulate resolution near zero crossings of the SDF. Consequently, it cannot guarantee precise geometry-radiance coupling in high-gradient regions. A tighter, geometry-aware criterion—such as bounding the gradient of transmittance or incorporating local curvature—would better justify the sampling strategy.

comments.title: SDF-to-density transformation assumes ideal gradient norm
comments.quote: we define the volume density function as Laplace’s cumulative distribution function (CDF) applied to a signed distance function (SDF) representation
comments.explanation:
The theoretical analysis in Theorem 1 relies on the assumption that the learned SDF satisfies \|
abla d_\Omega\| = 1 almost everywhere, which is only approximately enforced via the Eikonal loss. In practice, the gradient norm deviates from unity, especially off-surface or due to network approximation errors. This invalidates the derivative bound in equation (11), which depends critically on the exact SDF property. For instance, if \|
abla d\| < 1, the effective density falloff becomes sharper than modeled, breaking the error analysis in Lemma 2. The formulation should account for the observed gradient norm during inference, e.g., by normalizing the argument to the Laplace CDF as d(x)/( eta \|
abla d(x)\|), or the theory should be qualified as applying only in the ideal case of perfect Eikonal satisfaction.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 uses final sampling inconsistent with error-bound guarantee
comments.quote: Finally we return a fresh set of  $m = 64$  samples  $\hat{O}$  using inverse transform sampling (Line 11 in Algorithm 1).
comments.explanation: The algorithm constructs a dense sampling \mathcal{T} (up to 768 points) to satisfy the opacity error bound B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_+} \leq \epsilon, but then discards it and generates only m=64 samples via inverse CDF sampling. Since the error bound depends on sampling density, this coarse final sampling likely violates the \epsilon-accuracy guarantee. The paper provides no justification that 64 samples suffice, nor does it recompute the bound for the final set. This undermines the entire adaptive sampling argument. The final rendering should either use the refined \mathcal{T} or recompute and verify the bound for the new sample set.

comments.title: Bisection in Algorithm 1 lacks continuity justification
comments.quote: erefore the Mean Value Theorem implies the existence of  $\beta_{\star}\in (\beta ,\beta_{+})$  such that  $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{\star}} = \epsilon$  . We use the bisection method (with maximum of 10 iterations) to efficiently search for  $\beta_{\star}$  and update  $\beta_{+}$  accordingly (Lines 6
comments.explanation: The argument invokes the Mean Value Theorem incorrectly—existence of \beta_\star such that B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_\star} = \epsilon requires the Intermediate Value Theorem, not the Mean Value Theorem. More critically, this requires B_{\mathcal{T},\beta} to be continuous in \beta, which is not established. The bound depends on \widehat{R}(t_k), which involves multiplicative transmittance terms and discrete interval boundaries. Small changes in \beta can induce discontinuous jumps in accumulated values, especially with fixed \mathcal{T}. Without proof of continuity, the bisection method has no theoretical support. The authors should either prove continuity under fixed sampling or replace bisection with a direct search over a discretized \beta range.

comments.title: Upsampling criterion may undersample high-curvature regions
comments.quote: where the number of points sampled from each interval is proportional to its current error bound, equation 14.
comments.explanation: The upsampling strategy allocates new samples based on the interval-wise error bound, which includes \exp(-d_i^\star / \beta). This term decays rapidly with distance from the surface, so intervals moderately far from the zero level set—even if they contain high-curvature or thin structures—are downweighted. As a result, regions with rapid SDF variation but moderate |d_i^\star|, such as creases or filaments, may be undersampled. The criterion does not account for second-order SDF properties like Hessian norm or gradient variation, which are critical for resolving fine geometry. A curvature-aware refinement strategy would better ensure high-fidelity reconstruction.

comments.title: Final opacity estimate uses \beta while bound is computed with \beta_+
comments.quote: Either way, we use the final  $\mathcal{T}$  and  $\beta_{+}$  (guaranteed to provide  $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{+}}\leq \epsilon$  ) to estimate the current opacity  $\widetilde{O}$  Line 10 in Algorithm 1).
comments.explanation: The algorithm ensures B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_+} \leq \epsilon with \beta_+ > \beta, but the opacity \widetilde{O} is computed using \beta in the density function \sigma(x) = \alpha \Psi_\beta(-d_\Omega(x)). Since \beta < \beta_+, the actual density is higher than assumed during error estimation, potentially violating the \epsilon-bound. The bound computed with \beta_+ does not apply to the rendering with \beta. This creates a mismatch: the sampling is adapted to a different density profile than the one used in rendering. The algorithm should either use \beta_+ in \sigma(x) or ensure B_{\mathcal{T},\beta} \leq \epsilon before termination.

comments.title: Lemma 2 condition contradicts expected role of \beta
comments.quote:
Fix $n>0$. For any $\epsilon>0$ a sufficiently large $\beta$ that satisfies

$\beta\geq\frac{\alpha M^{2}}{4(n-1)\log(1+\epsilon)}$ (16)

will provide $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta}\leq\epsilon$.
comments.explanation: The condition suggests larger \beta reduces the error bound, but this contradicts the physical interpretation: increasing \beta broadens the density falloff, increasing integrated opacity and generally worsening rectangle rule error. Smaller \beta sharpens the density near the surface, which should improve localization and reduce error. As \epsilon \to 0, the bound requires \beta \gtrsim C / \epsilon, which is the opposite of the expected behavior. A correct derivation should likely yield \beta \lesssim f(\epsilon) or involve |\log \epsilon|. The current form cannot hold uniformly for small \epsilon and undermines the theoretical justification for the sampling strategy.

comments.title: Disentanglement claim lacks mechanistic justification
comments.quote: it allows efficient unsupervised disentanglement of shape and appearance in volume rendering
comments.explanation: The claim that the SDF-based density enables disentanglement is not supported by the method or analysis. Disentanglement depends on architectural isolation or explicit regularization, not merely on the form of the density function. The radiance field still depends on position and view direction through shared features (e.g., z(x)), and no evidence is provided that z(x) encodes appearance independently of geometry. The material transfer in Figure 7 could succeed due to feature co-adaptation rather than true disentanglement. A probe experiment—such as training a classifier to predict shape or material from z(x)—would strengthen the claim. Without such analysis, the disentanglement assertion is speculative.

comments.title: Incorrect comparison to NeRF++ in reconstruction performance
comments.quote: VolSDF produces more accurate surface reconstructions compared to NeRF [21] and  $\mathrm{NeRF}++$  [39], and comparable reconstruction compared to IDR [38], while avoiding the use of object masks.
comments.explanation: The claim that VolSDF outperforms NeRF++ is not substantiated. NeRF++ is designed for unbounded scenes with explicit background modeling, while VolSDF, like NeRF, is typically applied to bounded, object-centric setups (e.g., DTU). Direct comparison is only meaningful if NeRF++ is adapted to the same constrained domain. The paper does not present quantitative metrics for NeRF++ on DTU or BlendedMVS, nor does it clarify whether NeRF++ was evaluated under the same conditions. Without such evidence, the assertion appears premature. The claim should be revised to reflect comparisons only with methods evaluated on the same datasets under fair conditions.

comments.title: Opacity bound derivation assumes monotonicity without justification
comments.quote:
Finally, we can bound the opacity error for $t\in[t_{k},t_{k+1}]$ by noting that $\widehat{E}(t)$, and consequently also $\exp(\widehat{E}(t))$ are monotonically increasing in $t$, while $\exp(-\widehat{R}(t))$ is monotonically decreasing in $t$, and therefore

$\max_{t\in[t_{k},t_{k+1}]}\left|O(t)-\widehat{O}(t)\right|\leq\exp\left(-\widehat{R}(t_{k})\right)\left(\exp(\widehat{E}(t_{k+1}))-1\right).$ (14)
comments.explanation: The bound in equation (14) relies on the product of a decreasing and an increasing function being maximized at the endpoints, but this is not guaranteed. The function f(t) = \exp(-\widehat{R}(t))(\exp(\widehat{E}(t)) - 1) may achieve its maximum inside [t_k, t_{k+1}] if \widehat{R}(t) grows slowly while \widehat{E}(t) increases rapidly. Monotonicity of the components alone does not justify the bound. A valid argument would require convexity or additional structure of f(t), or the use of \sup_{t\in[t_k,t_{k+1}]} f(t). The current logic is insufficient to support the stated inequality.

comments.title: Radiance field uses unnormalized surface normals
comments.quote: Adding this dependency is motivated by the fact that BRDFs of common materials are often encoded with respect to the surface normal, facilitating disentanglement as done in surface rendering *[38]*.
comments.explanation:
The radiance field uses  m{n}(t) = 
abla_x d_\Omega( m{x}(t)), but BRDFs require unit normals. The Eikonal loss only encourages \|
abla d_\Omega\| \approx 1, and deviations are common, especially off-surface. Using unnormalized gradients introduces view-dependent artifacts and breaks physical consistency. The paper does not mention normalization. Redefining  m{n}(t) = 
abla_x d_\Omega / \|
abla_x d_\Omega\| would resolve this and align with standard practice in differentiable rendering.

comments.title: Chamfer Distance values for NeRF appear inconsistent
comments.explanation: NeRF's Chamfer Distance values (e.g., 5.44 for Scan 83) are implausibly high compared to IDR (1.33) and VolSDF (1.29), despite NeRF achieving high PSNR (30.65 dB), which indicates good view consistency. Such a large discrepancy suggests inconsistent mesh extraction—perhaps NeRF's geometry was not extracted at the standard 0-level set or used a different point cloud resolution. The evaluation should confirm that all methods use the same extraction threshold and post-processing to ensure fair comparison.

comments.title: Table 2 mislabels improvement metric
comments.explanation: The header 'Our Improvement (%)' suggests higher values are better, but Chamfer distance is a lower-is-better metric. A 91.2% value likely means VolSDF achieves 91.2% of NeRF++'s error, i.e., a 8.8% improvement, not a 91.2% reduction. This mislabeling severely misleads readers. The column should be labeled 'Chamfer Ratio (% of NeRF++)' or 'Relative Error' with a footnote clarifying the interpretation.

comments.title: Incorrect interval indexing in derivative bound
comments.quote: The benefit in Theorem 1 is that it allows to bound the density’s derivative in each interval $[t_{i},t_{i-1}]$ based only on the unsigned distance at the interval’s end points, $|d_{i}|,|d_{i+1}|$, and the density parameters $\alpha,\beta$.
comments.explanation: The interval $[t_i, t_{i-1}]$ is written in reverse order, contradicting the assumed increasing sequence $0 = t_1 < \cdots < t_n = M$. Theorem 1 and equation (12) clearly refer to $[t_i, t_{i+1}]$. The current indexing implies backward traversal, which is inconsistent with the integration direction and the definition of $\widehat{R}(t)$. This is a typographical error that undermines clarity and could mislead implementation.

comments.title: Convergence claim in \beta ignores non-uniformity near surface
comments.quote: As can be readily checked from this definition, as $\beta$ approach zero, the density $\sigma$ converges to a scaled indicator function of $\Omega$, that is $\sigma\to\alpha\mathbf{1}_{\Omega}$ for all points $\bm{x}\in\Omega\setminus\mathcal{M}$.
comments.explanation: The convergence $\sigma(\bm{x}) \to \alpha$ for $\bm{x} \in \Omega \setminus \mathcal{M}$ holds pointwise, but not uniformly in neighborhoods of $\mathcal{M}$. For points approaching the surface, convergence requires $\beta$ to shrink faster than the distance to $\mathcal{M}$. In rendering, rays may sample arbitrarily close to $\mathcal{M}$, so the rate of convergence matters. The statement should clarify that convergence is pointwise but not uniform near the boundary, as this affects numerical stability and sampling behavior.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces VolSDF, a novel approach for neural volume rendering that models density as a transformed signed distance function (SDF). The key contributions are: (1) a principled density parameterization using Laplace CDF applied to SDF, (2) theoretical bounds on opacity approximation error enabling accurate sampling, and (3) demonstrated disentanglement of geometry and appearance. The method shows improved geometry reconstruction over NeRF while maintaining comparable rendering quality, and achieves disentanglement capabilities not possible with standard NeRF architectures.

The paper presents a well-motivated approach to improving geometry reconstruction in neural volume rendering. The theoretical derivation of opacity bounds is sound and provides a strong foundation for the sampling algorithm. Empirical results demonstrate clear improvements in geometry quality over NeRF baselines while maintaining rendering fidelity. The disentanglement experiments provide compelling evidence for the method's inductive bias. However, several methodological gaps and unverified assumptions weaken the overall contribution.

Overview issues:

- Unverified SDF-to-density transformation assumptions: The paper assumes the Laplace CDF transformation of SDF to density works universally across all neural implicit representations without empirical validation. This assumption is critical to the method's success but lacks systematic testing. The authors should analyze how different SDF network architectures and regularization strengths affect the density transformation quality. Specifically, they should test whether the theoretical opacity bounds hold in practice across various SDF implementations and scene complexities, as the bounds' tightness directly impacts sampling accuracy and rendering quality.

- Opacity bound tightness not empirically validated: While the paper derives theoretical bounds on opacity approximation error (Theorems 1-2), it fails to demonstrate how tight these bounds are in practice. Figure 3 shows qualitative visualization but lacks quantitative analysis of the bound's tightness across different ray configurations and scene geometries. This is crucial because the sampling algorithm's efficiency depends on these bounds being reasonably tight. The authors should provide empirical measurements of the ratio between actual opacity error and the theoretical bound across diverse scenes, and analyze failure cases where the bounds are too loose.

- Sampling algorithm convergence not guaranteed: Algorithm 1 for sampling lacks a proof of convergence, which the authors acknowledge as a limitation. However, this is more than a minor issue - it's central to the method's reliability. The algorithm may fail to converge for certain scene configurations, leading to suboptimal sampling and degraded rendering quality. The paper should analyze convergence conditions empirically, identify failure modes, and provide fallback strategies. Without understanding when and why the algorithm fails, practitioners cannot reliably apply the method to new scenes.

- Insufficient geometry-appearance disentanglement analysis: The disentanglement experiments (Section 4.2, Figure 7) are limited to qualitative visual comparison. The paper lacks quantitative metrics to measure disentanglement quality and does not analyze how well the method separates geometry from appearance under varying lighting conditions or view-dependent effects. The claim that the SDF-based density provides better inductive bias for disentanglement needs stronger evidence through controlled experiments measuring cross-scene generalization and sensitivity to appearance variations.

- Limited analysis of grazing angle rendering bias: The paper does not specifically address how the proposed density transformation handles rendering bias near grazing angles, a known limitation in SDF-based volume rendering mentioned in the literature context. Given that the Laplace CDF smoothing is controlled by β, the authors should analyze how this affects accuracy when viewing directions are tangent to surfaces. This is particularly important for thin structures and high-frequency geometry where grazing angles are common.

- Missing synthetic validation of opacity bounds: The paper derives theoretical opacity approximation bounds in Theorems 1-2 but never validates them on controlled synthetic examples where ground truth is known. This is a significant gap because the sampling algorithm's efficiency depends on these bounds being reasonably tight. The authors should construct simple synthetic scenes (e.g., sphere, cube, torus) with known SDFs and analytically computable opacity, then measure the ratio between actual opacity error and the theoretical bound across different β values and sampling densities. Specifically, they should plot bound tightness as a function of distance from surface and identify regimes where the bounds become too loose to be useful.

- No worked example of density transformation: The paper introduces the SDF-to-density transformation in equation 2 but never computes it for a concrete, fully-specified model. Readers cannot calibrate their intuition about how the Laplace CDF smoothing affects density near surfaces. The authors should work through at least one standard geometric primitive - for example, compute the density field for a sphere of radius R with SDF d(x) = ||x|| - R, derive the analytical form of σ(x), and show how α and β parameters control the transition zone thickness. This would help readers understand the practical implications of the β parameter and its relationship to surface smoothness.

- Disentanglement lacks quantitative evaluation: The disentanglement claim in Section 4.2 is supported only by qualitative visual examples in Figure 7. For a paper that claims 'efficient unsupervised disentanglement' as a key benefit, this is insufficient. The authors should provide quantitative metrics measuring disentanglement quality, such as cross-scene reconstruction fidelity when swapping radiance fields, or metrics like mutual information between geometry and appearance features. They should also analyze failure cases - what types of scenes or materials fail to disentangle properly and why? Without quantitative evidence, the disentanglement claim remains anecdotal.

- Missing computational complexity analysis: The paper proposes a new sampling algorithm but provides no analysis of its computational cost compared to NeRF's hierarchical sampling. This is crucial for practitioners evaluating whether to adopt the method. The authors should benchmark runtime per ray, memory usage, and total training time against NeRF and IDR on identical hardware. They should also analyze how the algorithm's cost scales with scene complexity, β parameter, and error threshold ε. Without this analysis, readers cannot assess whether the theoretical benefits come at an unacceptable practical cost.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add quantitative analysis of opacity bound tightness across diverse scenes and ray configurations

- Provide empirical validation of SDF-to-density transformation under different network architectures and regularization strengths

- Analyze Algorithm 1 convergence conditions and identify failure modes with empirical evidence

- Include quantitative metrics for geometry-appearance disentanglement quality and test under varying lighting conditions

- Analyze and address rendering bias near grazing angles with specific experiments on thin structures

comments.title: Logical contradiction in algorithm update condition
comments.quote:
if  $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{+}} <   \epsilon$  then
6 Find  $\beta_{\star}\in (\beta ,\beta_{+})$  so that  $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{\star}} = \epsilon$
7 Update  $\beta_{+}\gets \beta_{\star}$
comments.explanation: The condition creates an impossible constraint. If $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{+}} < \epsilon$ and $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta}$ is decreasing in $\beta$, then for any $\beta_{\star} \in (\beta, \beta_{+})$, we have $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{\star}} > B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{+}} < \epsilon$, making $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{\star}} = \epsilon$ unattainable. The condition should be $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{+}} > \epsilon$ to enable finding a smaller $\beta_{\star}$ that achieves the target error.

comments.title: Bisection method convergence not guaranteed
comments.quote: We use the bisection method (with maximum of 10 iterations) to efficiently search for  $\beta_{\star}$  and update  $\beta_{+}$  accordingly (Lines 6 and 7 in Algorithm 1).
comments.explanation: Bisection requires $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta}$ to be monotonic in $\beta$ to guarantee convergence. However, $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta}$ involves terms like $\beta \exp(-\beta|\phi(t)|)$ which is not monotonic due to the competing effects of the linear $\beta$ factor and the exponential decay. Without monotonicity, bisection may fail to find $\beta_{\star}$. The authors should either prove monotonicity in their setting or replace bisection with a method that doesn't require it.

comments.title: Missing initialization of beta parameter
comments.quote:
Input: error threshold  $\epsilon >0$  .  $\beta$
1 Initialize  $\mathcal{T} = \mathcal{T}_0$
2 Initialize  $\beta_{+}$  such that  $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{+}}\leq \epsilon$
comments.explanation: The algorithm takes $\beta$ as input but never initializes it, yet uses it in the while condition $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta} > \epsilon$. This creates an undefined variable reference. Add 'Initialize $\beta$' after line 1 or clarify that $\beta$ is provided as an initial parameter value in the input specification.

comments.title: Undefined error bound function in algorithm
comments.quote:
Initialize  $\beta_{+}$  such that  $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{+}}\leq \epsilon$
3 while  $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta} > \epsilon$  and not max_iter do
comments.explanation: The algorithm uses $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta}$ throughout but never defines this error bound function. Without knowing how $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta}$ is computed from the sampling $\mathcal{T}$ and parameter $\beta$, the algorithm's correctness cannot be verified. Add a definition after the input section: 'Let $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta}$ denote the opacity error bound for sample set $\mathcal{T}$ with smoothing parameter $\beta$ as defined in equation (15)'.

comments.title: Inaccurate representation of DVR requirements
comments.quote: Recently, neural-based approaches such as DVR [22], IDR [38], NLR [14] have also been proposed to reconstruct scene geometry from multi-view images. However, these methods require accurate object masks and appropriate weight initialization due to the difficulty of propagating gradients.
comments.explanation: The claim that DVR requires accurate object masks is incorrect. DVR is a volume rendering approach that learns density directly from multi-view images without explicit segmentation. The mask requirement applies to IDR but not to DVR. This misrepresentation could mislead readers about the requirements of different neural reconstruction methods.

comments.title: Table 1 PSNR comparison incomplete
comments.explanation: The PSNR comparison only includes NeRF and VolSDF, but the Chamfer Distance comparison includes IDR, colmap2, colmap3, NeRF, and VolSDF. This creates an incomplete comparison - readers cannot assess how VolSDF's rendering quality compares to other geometry reconstruction baselines like IDR. Since the paper claims to compare favorably to relevant baselines, the PSNR metrics should include all methods shown in the Chamfer Distance row for a fair comparison.

comments.title: Inconsistent performance claim on BlendedMVS
comments.quote: Also in this case, we improve NeRF reconstructions considerably, while being on-par in terms of the rendering quality (PSNR).
comments.explanation: The claim of 'on-par' rendering quality contradicts the actual PSNR values in Table 2, where VolSDF has lower PSNR than NeRF++ in 8 out of 9 scenes. Only one scene shows higher PSNR. The authors should either qualify what 'on-par' means quantitatively (e.g., within X dB) or revise the claim to accurately reflect that VolSDF generally has slightly lower PSNR than NeRF++.

comments.title: Inconsistent NeRF radiance field comparison
comments.quote: For NeRF [21] we note that the radiance field is computed as  $L_{\psi}(z, v)$ , where  $L_{\psi}$  is a fully connected network with one hidden layer (of width 128 and ReLU activation) and  $z$  is a feature vector. We tested two versions of NeRF disentanglement: First, by switching the original radiance fields  $L_{\psi}$  of trained NeRF networks. Second, by switching the radiance fields of trained NeRF models with an identical radiance field model to ours, namely  $L_{\psi}(x, n, v, z)$
comments.explanation: The comparison is methodologically inconsistent. The first NeRF version uses $L_{\psi}(z, v)$ with a simple one-layer network, while the second version uses $L_{\psi}(x, n, v, z)$ which has different inputs and likely a different architecture. This makes the comparison unfair - you're testing different architectures entirely. To properly evaluate disentanglement, use the same NeRF architecture for both switching experiments and clearly specify whether the feature vector $z$ is also switched between scenes.

comments.title: Missing details on radiance field switching procedure
comments.quote: We have tested the disentanglement of scenes to geometry (density) and appearance (radiance field) by switching the radiance fields of two trained scenes. For VolSDF we switched  $L_{\psi}$ . For NeRF [21] we note that the radiance field is computed as  $L_{\psi}(z, v)$
comments.explanation: The description of how radiance fields are switched is incomplete. You state that you 'switch the radiance fields' but don't specify the exact procedure. For NeRF, since the radiance field depends on a feature vector $z$, it's unclear whether $z$ is also switched between scenes or remains tied to each scene's geometry network. This ambiguity makes it impossible to assess whether the comparison is fair. You should explicitly describe the switching procedure for each method.

comments.title: Contradiction about sampling algorithm correctness
comments.quote:
Furthermore, it allows to bound the opacity approximation error leading to high fidelity sampling of the volume rendering integral.

Some limitations of our method present interesting future research opportunities. First, although working well in practice, we do not have a proof of correctness for the sampling algorithm.
comments.explanation: There's a logical contradiction between claiming the method enables 'high fidelity sampling' in the first paragraph, then immediately stating in the limitations that 'we do not have a proof of correctness for the sampling algorithm.' If the algorithm's correctness is unproven, the claim of 'high fidelity sampling' is overstated. The authors should either qualify the high fidelity claim (e.g., 'empirically high fidelity') or remove it until theoretical guarantees can be established.

comments.title: Opacity error bound not formally established
comments.quote: Second, the particular form of the density as defined in equation 2 facilitates a bound on the error of the *opacity* (or, equivalently the *transparency*) of the rendered volume, a crucial component in the volumetric rendering pipeline.
comments.explanation: The paper claims the density definition 'facilitates a bound on the error of the opacity' but the formal results section (Section 3.3) does not actually provide or derive such a bound. While Theorems 1-2 bound the density derivative and opacity approximation error, these are not explicitly connected to an overall opacity error bound in the way claimed. The discussion in the conclusions section references this bound as if it were established, but this is not supported by the formal presentation.

comments.title: Algorithm termination condition insufficient
comments.quote: The algorithm runs iteratively until  $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta}\leq \epsilon$  or a maximal number of 5 iterations is reached.
comments.explanation: The termination condition $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta} \leq \epsilon$ may never be satisfied even with unlimited iterations. The algorithm only guarantees $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{+}} \leq \epsilon$ for $\beta_{+} > \beta$, but $\beta_{+}$ may converge to some value $\beta^* > \beta$ where $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta^*} = \epsilon$ but $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta} > \epsilon$. In this case, the algorithm would run indefinitely. The fixed iteration limit prevents infinite loops but doesn't address the fundamental issue. The authors should modify the termination condition to include a tolerance on $|\beta_{+} - \beta|$.

comments.title: Ambiguous sampling point distribution for Eikonal loss
comments.quote: the samples  $\pmb{z}$  are taken to combine a single random uniform space point and a single point from  $S$  for each pixel  $p$
comments.explanation: The expectation notation $\mathbb{E}_{\boldsymbol{z}}$ requires a clear probability distribution, but 'combine a single random uniform space point and a single point from $S$' is mathematically vague. Add the following sentence after the quoted text: 'Specifically, for each pixel $p$, we sample $\boldsymbol{z}_1 \sim \mathcal{U}(\text{bounding box})$ uniformly and $\boldsymbol{z}_2 \in S$ from the rendered surface points, then compute $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SDF}} = \frac{1}{2}[(\|\nabla d(\boldsymbol{z}_1)\| - 1)^2 + (\|\nabla d(\boldsymbol{z}_2)\| - 1)^2]$' to clarify that the expectation averages over these two point types.

comments.title: Missing statistical significance analysis
comments.quote: It can be observed that our method is on par with IDR (that uses object masks for all images) and outperforms NeRF and COLMAP in terms of reconstruction accuracy.
comments.explanation: The paper claims VolSDF compares 'favorably' to baselines, but VolSDF's mean Chamfer distance (0.86) is actually worse than colmap2 (0.65) in Table 1. While VolSDF outperforms NeRF (1.89) and IDR (0.90), the claim of favorable comparison is misleading without statistical analysis. The authors should either qualify their claim to specify which baselines VolSDF outperforms, or provide statistical tests to show whether the differences between methods are significant across the 15 scans.

comments.title: Overstated novelty compared to concurrent work
comments.quote:
o the difficulty of propagating gradients.

Independently from and concurrently with our work here, [27] also use implicit surface representation incorporated into volume rendering. In particular, they replace the local transparency function with an occupancy network [19]. This allows adding surface smoothing term to the loss, improving the quality of the resulting surfaces. Differently from their approach, we use signed distance representation, regularized with an Eikonal loss [38, 10] without any explicit smoothing term. Furthermore, we show that the choice of using signed distance allows bounding the opacity approximation error, facilitating the approximation of the volume rendering integral for the suggested family of densities.

<!-- PAGE
comments.explanation: The positioning relative to concurrent work [27] understates the similarities. Both methods use SDF-based volume rendering to address geometry extraction issues in NeRF. The claim that the key difference is the ability to bound opacity approximation error needs clarification - NeuS also provides theoretical guarantees about unbiased rendering. The distinction should focus on the specific density transformation and sampling approach rather than suggesting only VolSDF can bound opacity errors.

comments.title: Boundary convergence needs explicit specification
comments.quote: as $\beta$ approach zero, the density $\sigma$ converges to a scaled indicator function of $\Omega$, that is $\sigma\rightarrow\alpha\mathbf{1}_{\Omega}$ for all points $\bm{x}\in\Omega\setminus\mathcal{M}$.
comments.explanation: The convergence statement is incomplete because it doesn't specify behavior on the boundary $\mathcal{M}$. For $x\in\mathcal{M}$: $d_\Omega(x)=0$, so $\sigma(x)=\alpha\Psi_\beta(0)=\alpha/2$ for all $\beta>0$. As $\beta\to0$, $\sigma(x)\to\alpha/2$, not $\alpha\cdot1_\Omega(x)=\alpha$. Rewrite the statement as: 'as $\beta\to0$, $\sigma\to\alpha\cdot1_\Omega$ for all $x\in\Omega\setminus\mathcal{M}$, $\sigma\to\alpha/2$ for all $x\in\mathcal{M}$, and $\sigma\to0$ for all $x\notin\Omega$' to make the convergence complete.

comments.title: Inconsistent density-geometry relationship claim
comments.quote: We achieve that by modeling the volume density as a function of the geometry. This is in contrast to previous work modeling the geometry as a function of the volume density.
comments.explanation: This claim creates a false dichotomy. In neural volume rendering, density and geometry are inherently coupled - density represents geometry implicitly. Previous methods like NeRF also model density as a function of spatial position (which encodes geometry). The proposed SDF-to-density transformation is still modeling density as a function of spatial position, just with a specific functional form. Rewrite this to clarify that the key innovation is using an SDF-based density representation rather than claiming a fundamental reversal of the density-geometry relationship.

comments.title: Overstated disentanglement claim
comments.quote: This seemingly simple definition provides a useful inductive bias, allowing disentanglement of geometry (i.e., density) and radiance field, and improves the geometry approximation over previous neural volume rendering techniques.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims the SDF-to-density transformation 'allows disentanglement of geometry and radiance field' as if this is an established result. However, the paper only provides qualitative visual evidence without quantitative metrics. The technical sections do not prove that the SDF representation inherently enables better disentanglement. This claim should be softened to reflect that the method 'appears to facilitate' disentanglement based on empirical observations.

comments.title: Inconsistent citation format
comments.quote: This neural volume rendering approach, presented in *[21]* and developed by its follow-ups *[34, 2]* approximates the integral as alpha-composition in a differentiable way
comments.explanation: The citation format is inconsistent within the same sentence. The first reference uses brackets *[21]* while the follow-ups use *[34, 2]* with a comma. This creates visual inconsistency. The paper should use a consistent format throughout - either separate each reference with commas in all cases *[21, 34, 2]* or use the bracket format consistently for single references.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes VolSDF, a method for neural volume rendering that uses a signed distance function (SDF) to define density, aiming to improve geometry reconstruction quality and disentangle shape from appearance.

The paper makes a significant contribution by bridging the gap between implicit surface representation and volume rendering, effectively addressing the noisy geometry typical of earlier NeRF-based approaches. The theoretical derivation of the opacity error bound is a strong, original component that justifies the sampling strategy. The results demonstrate a clear improvement in surface reconstruction quality on standard datasets.

Overview issues:

- Unproven convergence of the sampling algorithm: Section 3.4 introduces an iterative sampling algorithm (Algorithm 1) to approximate the volume rendering integral, yet the paper acknowledges it lacks a proof of convergence. While the authors state that the algorithm works well in practice, the reliance on a heuristic bisection search for the parameter beta_star without theoretical guarantees for convergence or optimality is a potential vulnerability. For a method that claims to provide a 'guaranteed epsilon approximation' to the opacity, the lack of a formal proof for the sampling procedure undermines the rigor of the proposed framework. I suggest either providing a convergence proof under specific conditions on the SDF or characterizing the failure modes where the algorithm might provide sub-optimal samples.

- Implicit assumption of homogeneous density: The density formulation in Equation 2 assumes a constant density alpha inside the object, which is then smoothed by the Laplace CDF. This model inherently struggles with non-homogeneous objects or scenes with varying internal material properties. The authors briefly mention this as a limitation in Section 5, but the impact of this assumption on the disentanglement of radiance fields is not fully explored. If the radiance field is forced to compensate for the lack of internal density variation, the claim of 'unsupervised disentanglement' might be partially compromised. It would be beneficial to include an ablation or a discussion on how the model behaves when applied to objects with complex internal structures.

- Sensitivity to Eikonal loss weight: The training loss in Equation 17 includes an Eikonal loss term with a fixed hyper-parameter lambda = 0.1. The performance of SDF-based methods is notoriously sensitive to this regularization weight, as it balances the geometric constraint against the photometric loss. The paper does not provide an ablation study on the sensitivity of the reconstruction quality to this parameter. Without knowing how robust the results are to the choice of lambda, it is difficult to assess whether the method generalizes well to different object geometries or if the hyper-parameter requires extensive tuning per scene.

- Lack of comparison on thin or open surfaces: The methodology relies on the assumption that the scene is represented by an SDF, which inherently defines a closed, watertight manifold. Section 5 rightly identifies that representing non-watertight manifolds or zero-thickness surfaces is not possible with this formulation. However, the experimental section does not demonstrate the failure cases or the behavior of the model when confronted with such geometry. Including a qualitative analysis of how the model behaves when forced to reconstruct thin structures or open surfaces would clarify the practical boundaries of the proposed approach.

- Absence of a controlled synthetic experiment for disentanglement: The paper claims that VolSDF enables disentanglement of geometry and radiance fields, but this is only demonstrated qualitatively on real-world scenes where the ground truth is unknown. To validate this claim, the authors should perform a controlled experiment using a synthetic scene with known, decoupled ground-truth geometry and radiance components. Specifically, they should show that after training on two different synthetic objects, the model can successfully swap the radiance field of one with the geometry of the other while maintaining geometric fidelity. This would confirm that the disentanglement is a result of the proposed inductive bias rather than an artifact of the optimization process.

- Lack of quantitative analysis on the opacity sampling error: The paper derives a theoretical bound on the opacity approximation error in Section 3.3, yet it does not verify this bound empirically. It would be valuable to include a plot showing the actual observed opacity error versus the derived theoretical bound for a representative ray in a complex scene. This comparison is necessary to demonstrate that the bound is not only mathematically valid but also tight enough to be useful in practice for guiding the sampling algorithm.

- Missing ablation on the density transformation parameters: The density formulation relies on the Laplace CDF with learnable parameters alpha and beta. While the paper mentions that alpha = beta^-1 is used in implementation, it does not explore the impact of this coupling on the reconstruction of surfaces with varying sharpness. An ablation study comparing the reconstruction quality using this coupled parameterization versus independent optimization of alpha and beta would clarify whether this constraint is essential or if it limits the model's ability to represent diverse surface types.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a more formal analysis or a controlled simulation demonstrating the convergence behavior of the sampling algorithm in Algorithm 1.

- Add an ablation study on the Eikonal loss weight (lambda) to demonstrate the robustness of the reconstruction quality.

- Include a discussion or qualitative examples showing the model's behavior on non-watertight or thin-surface geometry to better delineate the method's scope.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for Eikonal loss sampling points
comments.quote: \mathcal {L} _ {\mathrm {S D F}} (\varphi) = \mathbb {E} _ {\boldsymbol {z}} \left(\left\| \nabla d (\boldsymbol {z}) \right\| - 1\right) ^ {2}
comments.explanation: The variable bm{z} is overloaded; it represents both the global geometry feature vector defined in Section 3.5 and the spatial sampling points for the Eikonal loss. This makes the loss function mathematically ambiguous. Please use a distinct symbol, such as bm{x}_{eik}, for the spatial sampling points and clarify that the expectation is taken over the union of the uniform distribution and the surface points.

comments.title: Undefined variables and missing input definitions in Algorithm 1
comments.quote:
.

Input: error threshold  $\epsilon >0$  .  $\bet
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 relies on several undefined components. The role of beta in the condition B_{T,beta} > epsilon is unclear given that beta_+ is initialized separately. Additionally, max_iter is used as a termination condition without being defined, and O_hat^{-1} in line 11 is not specified. Please explicitly define these input parameters and the inverse opacity function.

comments.title: Unjustified application of the Mean Value Theorem
comments.quote: . Therefore the Mean Value Theorem implies the existence of  $\beta_{\star}\in (\beta ,\beta_{+})$  such that  $B_{\mathcal{T},\beta_{\star}} = \epsi
comments.explanation: The Mean Value Theorem concerns derivatives and is not the correct tool for proving the existence of a root. The Intermediate Value Theorem is appropriate here, provided the error bound function B is continuous with respect to beta. Please justify the continuity of B or replace the reference to the Mean Value Theorem with an argument based on the Intermediate Value Theorem.

comments.title: Contradiction regarding the sampling algorithm's theoretical guarantees
comments.quote: Furthermore, it allows to bound the opacity approximation error leading to high fidelity sampling of the volume rendering integral.
comments.explanation: This claim is contradicted by the later admission that the authors do not have a proof of correctness for the sampling algorithm. One cannot claim a framework provides a guaranteed approximation if the algorithm lacks a proof. Please qualify this statement to reflect that the bound is derived, but the sampling strategy remains heuristic.

comments.title: Inconsistent interval notation for density derivative bound
comments.quote: The benefit in Theorem 1 is that it allows to bound the density’s derivative in each interval $[t_{i},t_{i-1}]$ based
comments.explanation: The interval [t_{i}, t_{i-1}] is ill-defined given the standard ordering t_0 < t_1 < ... < t_n. This appears to be a typographical error. Please rewrite this as [t_{i}, t_{i+1}] to maintain consistency with the definition provided in Theorem 1.

comments.title: Omission of NeuS as a concurrent work
comments.quote: Independently from and concurrently with our work here, [27] also use implicit surface representation incorporated into volume rendering.
comments.explanation: The literature review identifies [27] as the primary concurrent work, but NeuS (Wang et al., 2021) is a highly prominent concurrent study that also addresses unbiased volume rendering of SDFs. Failing to acknowledge NeuS provides an incomplete picture of the concurrent research landscape.

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of IDR requirements
comments.quote: However, these methods require accurate object masks and appropriate weight initialization due to the difficulty of propagating gradients.
comments.explanation: The claim that IDR requires accurate object masks is misleading. While the original paper used masks to simplify optimization, the method is not strictly dependent on them. Labeling mask requirements as a fundamental limitation ignores the method's capability to operate on unmasked data.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes VolSDF, a neural volume rendering framework that models density as a transformed signed distance function. The approach introduces a theoretical bound on opacity approximation error to guide adaptive ray sampling and claims improved geometry reconstruction and geometry-appearance disentanglement. While the core idea is promising and the experimental results on DTU and BlendedMVS are competitive, the manuscript contains significant theoretical gaps and missing empirical analyses that must be addressed before publication.

The paper makes a clear and valuable contribution by linking SDF-based density parameterization to a principled opacity error bound. The experimental evaluation on standard multi-view datasets demonstrates competitive surface reconstruction and novel view synthesis. The disentanglement experiments are visually compelling and align with the theoretical motivation. However, the theoretical claims lack computational feasibility checks, the sampling algorithm relies on unproven heuristics, and the empirical validation of key claims remains qualitative.

Overview issues:
